title: Multi-LogiEval: Towards Evaluating Multi-Step Logical Reasoning Ability of Large Language Models
authors: Nisarg Patel, Mohith Kulkarni, Mihir Parmar, Aashna Budhiraja, Mutsumi Nakamura, Neeraj Varshney, Chitta Baral (Arizona State University, USA)
venue: EMNLP 2024 Main Conference (per arXiv abstract page; extraction header notes 'accepted; per arXiv abstract page comments')
claim: LLMs (GPT-4, ChatGPT, Gemini-Pro, Yi-34B, Orca-2-13B, Mistral-7B) show a significant, systematic drop in multi-step logical reasoning accuracy as reasoning depth increases from 1 to 5 on a new benchmark spanning propositional, first-order, and non-monotonic logic (average accuracy ~68% at depth-1 to ~43% at depth-5, per Abstract/Conclusion). Non-monotonic (NM) reasoning shows the opposite trend, actually increasing from ~35.63% at d1 to ~55.83% at d5 (Sec 4.2), and manual reasoning-chain analysis reveals that longer chains do not correlate with better outcomes and that error propagation, not lack of any single-step competence, drives the depth-wise degradation.
novelty: The authors position Multi-LogiEval as the first dataset to jointly cover (1) genuinely multi-step reasoning (depths 1-5, not just single-step as in ProntoQA/LogicBench), (2) three logic types together -- propositional (PL), first-order (FOL), and non-monotonic (NM) -- and (3) a broad rule inventory (33 base inference rules, >60 combinations across depths), which prior benchmarks individually lack (Table 1 comparison vs LogicNLI, ProofWriter, FOLIO, SimpleLogic, ProntoQA, RuleTaker, LogicBench; Sec 2 Related Work). They explicitly contrast with ProntoQA (single-step modus ponens in FOL only), FOLIO (multi-step but FOL-only), RuleTaker (mainly modus ponens), LogicBench (broad rules but single-step only), and FLD (formal-logic multi-step but PL/FOL only, no NM) (Sec 2).

protocol.description: Primary evaluation is single-shot: each model receives one <prompt, context, question> triplet per instance and must output a binary Yes/No label in a single zero-shot Chain-of-Thought (Zero-shot-CoT) call (Sec 4.1: 'To evaluate any LLMs, we provide <p,c,q> as input to predict a label Yes or No'). A secondary few-shot (3-shot) variant is reported in Appendix G as a fixed-demonstration single-shot call (not an interactive/multi-turn loop): 'we also evaluate LLMs in a 3-shot setting (results are in Appendix G)' (Sec 4.1). A separate neuro-symbolic pipeline (Sec 4.3) has GPT-4 translate NL context+question into a formal executable program that is then solved by the Prover9 theorem prover -- this is a two-stage single-shot-per-stage pipeline, not an interactive multi-turn dialogue with the LLM itself.
protocol.observation_content: Full problem statement in one call: a natural-language 'story' context embedding the logical premises plus a yes/no entailment question, e.g. Sec 4.1: 'each context and question pair is created so that the conclusion provided in the question always entails context... We prompt the model to assign a label Yes if the conclusion logically entails the context; otherwise, No.' Zero-shot-CoT means no worked demonstrations are shown ('we can not expect in-context examples of inference rules for various reasoning depths will always be available in prompts', Sec 1); the model reasons and answers in one pass. The 3-shot variant (Appendix G) instead shows 3 worked demonstrations before the target instance in the same single call. Exact literal prompt template text for the main-experiment inference call is not reproduced in this local extraction (the extraction preserves the data-generation prompt structure in Sec 3.1.2/Fig.3/Appendix C, and states 'The prompt used for experiments is provided below' in Sec 4.1, but the literal follow-on prompt string is not captured in the extraction) -- marked unverified — missing from extraction.

representations.note: The paper does not compare alternative input/representation formats (e.g., natural language vs. symbolic/logical notation) for the same instances in the main results; it only compares zero-shot-CoT vs 3-shot demonstration framing (Sec 4.1 vs Appendix G/Table 24) and, separately, an NL-to-formal-program translation pipeline for a neuro-symbolic ablation (Sec 4.3/Table 7). All primary-benchmark instances are presented as natural-language story context + natural-language question (Sec 3.1.2, Table 4); no raw symbolic-formula representation is tested against models directly.
representations.zero_shot_vs_3shot: 3-shot prompting raises average accuracy substantially across depths and logics versus zero-shot-CoT (compare Table 6 vs Table 24), e.g. GPT-4 PL d5 rises from 66.67% (Table 6) to 73.33% (Table 24); GPT-4 FOL d4 rises from 59.17% to 68.33%. Appendix G: 'we observe notable improvements in the performance of various LLMs... the observed performance drop from d4 to d5 in open-source models comparable across both settings, suggesting that while few-shot examples enhance overall accuracy, they do not fully mitigate the inherent challenges these models face in higher depths.'
representations.nl_to_formal_program: Sec 4.3/Table 7: converting FOL instances to Prover9-executable form (via GPT-4, following the Logic-LM/Pan et al. 2023 approach) yields overall accuracy that does NOT monotonically track depth the way direct NL prompting does: 55.83% (d1), 46.67% (d2), 38.89% (d3), 40.00% (d4), 60.00% (d5), with Executable Rate ~76-86% and Executable Accuracy 30-63% (Table 7) -- 'at d5, both overall accuracy and executable accuracy show a significant improvement' (Sec 4.3), an atypical, non-monotone pattern versus the direct-NL results in Table 6.

models.name: GPT-4
models.key_results: Table 6 (zero-shot-CoT): PL d1=89.17%, d2=69.52%, d3=82.22%, d4=71.67%, d5=66.67%; FOL d1=83.85%...d5=66.67%; NM d1=36.88%...d5=60.00%. Table 24 (3-shot): PL d1=90.00%...d5=73.33%; FOL d1=97.78%...d5=73.33%; NM d1=54.38%...d5=75.00%. Appendix H: multi-variable FOL (n-ary relations, single-step only) average accuracy 80%.

models.name: ChatGPT
models.key_results: Table 6: PL d1=91.67%...d5=44.44%; FOL d1=97.69%...d5=37.78%; NM d1=33.75%...d5=60.00%. Table 24 (3-shot): PL d5=80.00%; FOL d5=64.44%; NM d5=45.00%. Reasoning-chain length analysis (Sec 4.3): average chain length at d5 for PL/FOL = 13.85 (longest among the three top proprietary models). Appendix H: multi-variable FOL average accuracy 84.3%.

models.name: Gemini-Pro
models.key_results: Table 6: PL d1=90.00%...d5=60.00%; FOL d1=76.92%...d5=53.33%; NM d1=46.25%...d5=60.00%. Table 24 (3-shot): PL d5=77.78%; FOL d5=57.78%; NM d5=75.00%. Reasoning-chain length at d5: 8.85 (shortest of the three proprietary models, Sec 4.3). Appendix H: multi-variable FOL average accuracy 90%.

models.name: Yi-34B (Yi-34B-Chat)
models.key_results: Table 6: PL d1=85.00%...d5=26.67%; FOL d1=90.00%...d5=13.33% (second-lowest FOL-d5 score in the table, after Orca-13B's 6.67%); NM d1=37.50%...d5=65.00%. Table 24 (3-shot) PL d5=64.44%, FOL d5=51.11%, NM d5=50.00%. Sec 4.2: 'larger open-source models demonstrate decreasing performance' relative to smaller Mistral-7B; Sec 4.3: Yi-34B only marginally better (1.5%) than Mistral-7B at d3.

models.name: Orca-2-13B
models.key_results: Table 6: PL d1=75.83%...d5=15.56% (lowest PL-d5 in table); FOL d1=66.92%...d5=6.67% (lowest score in entire table); NM d1=21.88%...d5=25.00%. Sec 4.3: at d5, Mistral-7B (smallest of the three open models) 'outperformed Orca-13B by achieving 3x performance despite Orca-13B's larger size.'

models.name: Mistral-7B (Mistral-7B-Instruct)
models.key_results: Table 6: PL d1=80.83%...d5=44.44%; FOL d1=83.85%...d5=20.00%; NM d1=37.50%...d5=65.00%. Sec 4.3: 'Mistral-7B, the smallest, performed best across various depths of classical logic, except at the simplest d1... consistently outperformed Orca-13B and Yi-34B.'

headline_numbers: Abstract/Conclusion: average accuracy across evaluated LLMs drops from ~68% at depth-1 to ~43% at depth-5 for classical (PL/FOL) and non-classical logic combined.

Table 6: PL average accuracy by depth = 85.42% (d1), 60.79% (d2), 61.61% (d3), 55.83% (d4), 42.96% (d5); FOL average = 83.21%, 59.84%, 58.79%, 51.39%, 32.96% (d1-d5); NM average = 35.63%, 41.02%, 51.67%, 51.67%, 55.83% (d1-d5) -- NM trend is INCREASING with depth, opposite PL/FOL.

Sec 4.2: random baseline per depth (Acc_random = p_yes^2 + p_no^2) = 86.63% (d1), 67.35% (d2), 53.71% (d3), 58.33% (d4), 83.33% (d5); models on average perform BELOW this random baseline at several depths ('these models perform lower in terms of average accuracy compared to the random baseline').

Sec 4.3: reasoning-chain error analysis -- ~27.4% of incorrect d2 chains and ~22% of incorrect d3 chains stem from failure to correctly map information from context/premises to conclusion; average reasoning-chain length at d5 for PL/FOL is 13.85 (ChatGPT), 10.87 (GPT-4), 8.85 (Gemini), with longer chains not correlating with better accuracy.

Table 7 (neuro-symbolic GPT-4 + Prover9, FOL only): Overall Acc 55.83/46.67/38.89/40.00/60.00% and Executable Acc 51.46/47.83/30.00/41.43/62.86% for d1-d5 -- a non-monotonic pattern distinct from the direct-NL results.

Appendix F: data-quality/error rates from human validation -- Incorrect Logical Premises ~14.3% (223/1552), Leaking Conclusion ~3.7% (57/1552), Repetition of Samples ~3.7% (57/1552); per-logic-type correction rates PL ~22% (115/525), FOL ~19% (102/535), NM ~25.9% (127/492); per-depth correction rates 17.8%/21%/23.5%/33.2%/21% for d1-d5.

Appendix I: human evaluation on a 75-instance subset yields average accuracy d1=75.56%, d2=64.71%, d3=64.44%, d4=66.67%, d5=64.44% (inter-annotator agreement 0.853) -- humans also degrade from d1 but plateau lower rather than collapsing further at d4/d5 the way several LLMs do.
Table 5: final dataset size 1,552 instances total (PL 525, FOL 535, NM 492) across depths 1-5 (410/442/310/280/110 instances respectively); 1,126 'Yes' vs 426 'No' gold labels.
failure_modes: Error propagation: mistakes made in the initial reasoning step propagate through the chain, driving the PL/FOL accuracy collapse at d4-d5 (Sec 1, Sec 4.3: 'longer reasoning steps are more prone to error propagation at later stages, causing the models to deviate further from the true conclusion').

Wrong interpretation/mapping of evidence: 'these models reach incorrect conclusions often due to the wrong interpretation of evidence' and specifically fail 'to correctly map information, either from context to conclusion or the premise from one step to the next step' (~27.4% of wrong d2 chains, ~22% of wrong d3 chains) (Sec 4.3).

Longer reasoning chains do not correlate with correctness: ChatGPT generates longer chains than GPT-4/Gemini at d5 (13.85 vs 10.87 vs 8.85 average length) yet this does not translate into better accuracy (Sec 4.3).

FOL predicate misassignment: 'generic rules in FOL contexts lead to deviations from the correct reasoning path... it assigns predicates incorrectly to the FOL inference rule,' most prominent at d5, contributing to a ~10-point average PL-vs-FOL performance gap at that depth (Sec 4.3).

Difficulty with implicit/assumption-only inference: 'models have difficulty concluding based solely on assumptions present in the context when explicit knowledge is absent' (Sec 4.3), relevant especially at d1 where interpreting assumptions is described as crucial.

Model-scale non-monotonicity among open-source models: larger open-source models (Yi-34B, Orca-13B) underperform the smaller Mistral-7B at higher depths -- 'larger open-source models demonstrate decreasing performance' (Sec 4.2), with Mistral-7B achieving '3x performance' over Orca-13B at d5 for PL/FOL despite Orca-13B's larger size (Sec 4.3).

Multi-variable/n-ary FOL generation difficulty: 'creating natural language instances is challenging for this kind of setup' for FOL rules involving three or more variables (Sec 4.3, Appendix H) -- these were evaluated only at single-step depth as a preliminary study.

Below-random performance: several LLMs perform below the computed random baseline at multiple depths, per Sec 4.2, indicating systematic (not just noisy) reasoning failure rather than mere uncertainty.
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's central design and finding are about depth (multi-step chains within a single forward pass), not about interactive/multi-turn protocol. In a single zero-shot-CoT call, models can reliably do depth-1 (single-rule) inference (PL avg 85.42%, FOL avg 83.21%, Table 6) but their accuracy on PL/FOL degrades sharply as more chained inference rules must be applied within that same one-shot generation (down to 42.96%/32.96% at depth-5). Adding demonstrations (3-shot, still single-shot per instance, Appendix G/Table 24) substantially raises accuracy at every depth (e.g., GPT-4 FOL d4 59.17% to 68.33%) but 'they do not fully mitigate the inherent challenges these models face in higher depths' for open-source models, i.e., few-shot demonstrations improve absolute performance without eliminating the depth-driven degradation. There is no interactive/iterative-feedback loop tested anywhere in the paper (no multi-turn correction, no verifier-in-the-loop dialogue) -- the closest the paper comes to a multi-stage process is the two-stage neuro-symbolic pipeline (Sec 4.3), which is a fixed pipeline (LLM translates once, solver executes once), not an interactive loop with the LLM under test.

relation_to_candidate.position: Multi-LogiEval is entirely single-shot at the model-under-test level: each instance is one <prompt, context, question> triplet answered with a single Yes/No token per call (Sec 4.1), with a fixed-demonstration (3-shot) variant that is still one call per instance, not an interactive/multi-turn loop. The model observes the full natural-language story context plus the query at once; there is no partial/turn-by-turn feedback. The output shape is a single binary label (Yes/No) per instance -- narrower than c18's three-way True/False/Unknown, since Multi-LogiEval treats NM's open-world 'defeasible' cases as part of its own labeling scheme rather than emitting an explicit Unknown class, and does not require closed-world-vs-open-world distinction at the label level the way ProofWriter/c18 do. The verifier is an exact-match string comparison against a human-validated gold label (manually checked in Appendix F for logical correctness), not an independent theorem-prover check in the main pipeline -- though the paper does run a separate neuro-symbolic ablation (GPT-4 to Prover9, Sec 4.3/Table 7) that substitutes a real prover as an alternative verdict source for a FOL subset.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c18: Both operate on synthetic, rule-based theories (facts + if-then/inference rules) at controlled proof depth (1-5 in both), score exact-match against a generator-verified gold label, and are explicitly motivated by the same lineage of single-step-limited benchmarks (ProntoQA is cited by both as the single-step precedent to move beyond, Sec 2 here / c18's 'Literature parents'). Both treat depth as the primary latent difficulty dial and report depth ladders as the main results table. Both note contamination-resistance concerns are relevant to synthetic generation, although Multi-LogiEval uses realistic entity labels/domains (education, finance, wildlife photography) rather than c18's fully fictional/nonce predicates ('wumpus'/'yumpus'-style), so Multi-LogiEval is less contamination-resistant by construction (see Sec 3.1.2: entities are 'actual entities' from real domains, not nonce symbols).
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Label space: Multi-LogiEval is strictly binary Yes/No entailment; c18 requires three-way True/False/Unknown, which is the ProofWriter/open-world extension Multi-LogiEval does not adopt. (2) Ontology/contamination: Multi-LogiEval generates naturalistic story contexts over real-world domains via few-shot-prompted Claude-2 (Sec 3.1.2), not fictional/nonce predicates -- so it is more susceptible to prior-knowledge shortcuts than c18's fictional-ontology design. (3) Rule scope: Multi-LogiEval's primary contribution is breadth of inference-rule TYPES (33 base rules, 60+ combinations across PL/FOL/NM, Table 2/Sec 3.1), whereas c18 (via PrOntoQA/RuleTaker) is built around a narrower, simpler core rule set (chaining modus-ponens-like rules with distractors) but adds a full CWA/OWA Unknown semantics and a constraint-satisfaction stratum that Multi-LogiEval lacks entirely. (4) Generation and reproducibility: Multi-LogiEval's dataset is a fixed, static, human-validated release (~1.6k instances, generated once via Claude-2 + manual correction, Sec 3.3/Appendix F) rather than a live, reseedable generator; c18's PrOntoQA-based harness is run-verified to regenerate fresh, differently-seeded instances deterministically with no model call, which Multi-LogiEval's story-generation-then-fixed-release pipeline does not offer. (5) Verifier: Multi-LogiEval's main-track gold labels come from human validation of LLM-generated instances (Appendix F), not from an automated proof engine; only the smaller neuro-symbolic ablation (Sec 4.3) introduces an actual solver (Prover9) as a checkable oracle, and only for FOL. c18's PrOntoQA generator gold label comes directly from an automated proof engine at generation time (per c18.md), which is a stronger automatic-verifier guarantee than Multi-LogiEval provides in its primary pipeline.
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: Beyond Multi-LogiEval, c18 would add: (a) a three-way True/False/Unknown label under explicit open-world semantics, testing whether models correctly withhold judgment absent sufficient information -- a distinction Multi-LogiEval's binary Yes/No does not probe; (b) fully fictional/nonce predicates that eliminate the real-world-prior shortcut Multi-LogiEval's naturalistic domains still allow; (c) a live, reseedable, deterministic generator (verified byte-identical across runs) enabling unlimited fresh, uncontaminated test instances rather than a fixed released set; (d) an automated proof-engine gold label (and failed-inference-step diagnosis) rather than reliance on human-validated static annotations; and (e) a constraint-satisfaction-puzzle stratum entirely outside Multi-LogiEval's inference-rule-chaining paradigm.

corpus_claim: Multi-LogiEval (Patel et al., EMNLP 2024 Main) is a depth-1-to-5 multi-step logic benchmark over 60+ inference-rule combinations (propositional/FOL/non-monotonic); a relevant depth-ladder reference.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper confirms every element of this claim in its own text: venue is EMNLP 2024 Main Conference (per extraction header); Table 5 and Sec 3.3 confirm reasoning depths 1 through 5; the Introduction states '33 inference rules and reasoning patterns and more than 60 complex combinations of these inference rules with a different number of reasoning steps (1∼5)'; Sec 1/Abstract confirm coverage of propositional (PL), first-order (FOL), and non-monotonic (NM) logic types. The characterization as 'a relevant depth-ladder reference' is also accurate -- Table 6/Sec 4.2 present exactly a depth-wise accuracy ladder (d1 through d5) as the paper's central result, directly comparable to c18's D0-D5 depth-bin design. No correction needed.
baseline_relevant: Instance sizes/difficulty dials: depth bins 1-5, with dataset sizes per depth of 410 (d1), 442 (d2), 310 (d3), 280 (d4), 110 (d5) instances (Table 5), unevenly distributed and shrinking sharply at higher depths -- useful caution that depth-5 slices can be small (n=110 total across all three logic types) and thus noisier for per-model accuracy estimates.

Rule/distractor composition as difficulty dial: depth is operationalized as chained inference-rule combinations (each step = one inference rule; up to 5 chained rules), not distractor injection per se -- no explicit 'irrelevant distractor' rule stratum is described as in RuleTaker/ProofWriter/PrOntoQA-style generators; Multi-LogiEval's difficulty dial is purely the rule-chain length/combination, per Sec 3.1.1.

Prompt format effects on accuracy: zero-shot-CoT (primary) vs 3-shot demonstrations (Appendix G) -- 3-shot substantially raises accuracy at every depth for every model (e.g., GPT-4 NM d1 36.88% to 54.38%; Table 6 vs Table 24), confirming that adding worked demonstrations is a strong lever, though it does not eliminate the depth-4/5 degradation for smaller open-source models.

Metric: simple binary-label accuracy (exact match on Yes/No), computed per depth per logic type (Table 6); paper also computes a random baseline per depth (Acc_random = p_yes^2 + p_no^2, Sec 4.2) as an important floor comparator -- baselines range 53.71% to 86.63% depending on depth's class balance, and several models score BELOW this floor, a useful sanity-check metric to replicate.

Floors/ceilings observed: PL/FOL average accuracy ceiling ~83-85% at d1, floor ~33-43% at d5 (Table 6 Avg row); NM shows the opposite -- floor ~35.63% at d1, rising to ~55.83% at d5; human performance (Appendix I, n=75 subset) sits at 75.56% (d1) to 64.44% (d5), notably lower than top LLMs at d1 but higher than several LLMs at d4/d5, suggesting human accuracy is a relevant floor/ceiling reference, not a fixed 100% ceiling.

Contamination/data-release status: Multi-LogiEval is fully released (data at https://github.com/Mihir3009/Multi-LogiEval) as a static, human-validated dataset generated once via few-shot-prompted Claude-2 plus manual correction (Sec 3.1.2, Appendix F) -- it is NOT a live reseedable generator, and its NL contexts use realistic domains/entities (not fictional/nonce symbols), which is a contamination-risk contrast worth flagging against c18's fictional/reseedable design.

Data-quality/error-rate baseline: even after human validation, Appendix F reports the underlying generation process required manual correction of ILP/LC/RS errors in ~14.3%/3.7%/3.7% of the raw 1552 samples respectively, and depth-wise correction rates rose with depth (17.8% at d1 to 33.2% at d4) -- useful evidence that LLM-generated synthetic instances at higher depth require nontrivial validation/correction effort, relevant to designing an un-optimized baseline generator pipeline.

Executable/neuro-symbolic ablation as an alternate verifier baseline: Table 7's Prover9-based pipeline gives an independent, solver-checked accuracy and an 'Executable Rate' (grammar correctness, 76.67-85.83%) plus 'Executable Accuracy' (semantic correctness of executable samples, 30.00-62.86%) -- a useful reference for how much accuracy a formal-solver verifier recovers/loses versus direct LLM NL answering on the same FOL instances.
quotes: Abstract: "Experimental results show that there is a significant drop in the performance of LLMs as the reasoning steps/depth increases (average accuracy of ∼68% at depth-1 to ∼43% at depth-5)."
Sec 4.1 (protocol): "To evaluate any LLMs, we provide <p,c,q> as input to predict a label Yes or No where p is a natural language prompt... Each model is evaluated in a zero-shot-CoT setting."

Sec 1: "our proposed dataset provides ∼1.6k high-quality instances that cover 33 inference rules and reasoning patterns and more than 60 complex combinations of these inference rules with a different number of reasoning steps (1∼5)."

Sec 4.2: "The random baselines for depths d1, d2, d3, d4, and d5, with corresponding Acc_random of 86.63%, 67.35%, 53.71%, 58.33%, and 83.33%, respectively... these models perform lower in terms of average accuracy compared to the random baseline."
Sec 4.3: "Longer reasoning chains do not necessarily correlate with better reasoning outcomes, highlighting the complexity of complex reasoning task."
Sec 4.2: "In addition, larger open-source models demonstrate decreasing performance."
Sec 4.3: "Mistral-7B outperformed Orca-13B by achieving 3x performance despite Orca-13B's larger size."

Appendix G: "we observe notable improvements in the performance of various LLMs [in 3-shot]... the observed performance drop from d4 to d5 in open-source models comparable across both settings, suggesting that while few-shot examples enhance overall accuracy, they do not fully mitigate the inherent challenges these models face in higher depths."
Appendix F: "We found ∼14.3% (223 samples) of the total 1552 samples with ILP, ∼3.7% (57 samples) with LC, and ∼3.7% (57 samples) with RS."

corrections: protocol.mode changed from 'both' to 'single-shot': the paper has no interactive/multi-turn evaluation track at all. Zero-shot-CoT (primary), 3-shot (Appendix G), and the neuro-symbolic GPT-4-to-Prover9 pipeline (Sec 4.3) are all single-shot-per-instance calls; this is confirmed by the dossier's own protocol.description, one_shot_vs_multistep, and relation_to_candidate.position fields, which explicitly state 'There is no interactive/iterative-feedback loop tested anywhere in the paper.' The prior 'both' value contradicted the dossier's own analysis.

models[Yi-34B].key_results: removed false claim that Yi-34B's FOL d5=13.33% is the 'lowest FOL-d5 score in the table.' Per Table 6, Orca-13B's FOL d5=6.67% is lower (and is correctly identified elsewhere in the same dossier, in models[Orca-2-13B], as 'lowest score in entire table'). Corrected to 'second-lowest FOL-d5 score in the table, after Orca-13B's 6.67%.'

Fixed section misattribution: the quote 'larger open-source models demonstrate decreasing performance' is from Sec 4.2 (Main Results), not Sec 4.3 (Analysis and Discussion) as the dossier had stated in three places (models[Yi-34B].key_results, failure_modes, quotes). Split the previously merged quotes[] entry (which had spliced a Sec 4.2 sentence and a Sec 4.3 sentence together under a single 'Sec 4.3' label) into two separately-attributed entries, and corrected the section label in models[Yi-34B].key_results and failure_modes accordingly. The '3x performance...Orca-13B's larger size' quote is correctly from Sec 4.3.
